attack_patterns.name: Brute Force
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may use brute force techniques to gain access to accounts when passwords are unknown or when password hashes are obtained. Brute forcing credentials involves systematically guessing passwords by trying every possible combination or using dictionary-based approaches. This technique is one of the oldest and most common attack vectors against authentication systems, often automated with tools like Hydra, Medusa, or custom scripts. Without proper account lockout policies and monitoring, brute force attacks can be highly effective against weak passwords.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor authentication logs for high volumes of failed login attempts from single or distributed sources. Set alert thresholds at 5+ failed attempts per account within 10 minutes and 50+ failed attempts across accounts from a single IP within 1 hour. Correlate Windows Event IDs 4625 (failed logon) and 4771 (Kerberos pre-authentication failed) with firewall and VPN logs. Monitor Azure AD Sign-in logs for errorCode 50126 (invalid credentials) with frequency analysis. Deploy SIEM correlation rules for distributed brute force across multiple accounts from the same source network.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: SolarWinds (2020) attackers used password spraying before supply chain compromise. Microsoft reported Midnight Blizzard (Nobelium) used residential proxy-based password spraying in January 2024 to compromise a legacy test tenant account without MFA.

attack_patterns.name: Brute Force: Password Guessing
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may guess passwords to attempt access to accounts without prior knowledge of system passwords. Password guessing differs from brute force exhaustive search in that it uses targeted lists of common passwords, default credentials, and contextually likely passwords based on OSINT about the target organization. Attackers often compile lists from leaked password databases, organizational naming conventions, and social media profiles of employees. This technique is particularly effective against organizations that do not enforce password complexity requirements or allow commonly used passwords.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for multiple failed authentication attempts against single accounts. Correlate with Windows Event ID 4625 (Logon Type 2, 3, 10) and Linux auth.log 'Failed password' entries. Track source IPs across failed attempts to detect slow-and-low guessing that stays under lockout thresholds. Implement real-time alerting on 3+ failed attempts to service accounts or privileged accounts within 5 minutes. Monitor Azure AD for riskEventType 'unfamiliarFeatures' and 'anonymizedIPAddress' in sign-in risk detections.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT28 (Fancy Bear) used password guessing with default credentials to access email servers. The 2014 iCloud celebrity photo breach used targeted password guessing against Apple accounts.

attack_patterns.name: Brute Force: Password Cracking
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may use password cracking techniques to recover plaintext passwords from captured credential material such as password hashes, encrypted passwords, or Kerberos tickets. Tools like Hashcat and John the Ripper can perform offline cracking using GPU acceleration, making even complex passwords vulnerable if the underlying hash algorithm is weak (e.g., NTLM, MD5, SHA-1 without salting). This technique is performed offline and is therefore undetectable by the target system's authentication monitoring. The effectiveness depends on password complexity, hash algorithm strength, and available computational resources.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Password cracking occurs offline and cannot be directly detected. Focus detection on the prerequisite credential theft: monitor for unauthorized access to SAM database, NTDS.dit, /etc/shadow, or LSASS process memory. Alert on Windows Event ID 4661 (handle requested to SAM) and 4662 (directory service access). Monitor for suspicious use of ntdsutil.exe, secretsdump, or mimikatz. Post-cracking detection: correlate new logon events (Event ID 4624) from unusual sources following known credential exposure incidents.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The LinkedIn 2012 breach exposed 117 million SHA-1 hashed passwords (unsalted), most of which were cracked within days. The Dropbox 2012 breach similarly exposed bcrypt-hashed credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Brute Force: Password Spraying
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may use a single or small list of commonly used passwords against many different accounts to attempt to acquire valid credentials. Password spraying inverts traditional brute force by trying one password across many accounts rather than many passwords against one account, effectively evading account lockout mechanisms. This technique is highly effective against organizations using common password patterns and is a favored method of nation-state actors targeting cloud and enterprise environments. Attackers often use residential proxies to distribute attempts across many IP addresses.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for a pattern of single failed authentication attempts across many accounts from the same or similar source IP ranges within a short time window. Alert on 10+ unique accounts failing authentication with the same source IP within 30 minutes. Correlate Azure AD Sign-in logs for errorCode 50126 with IP reputation feeds. Monitor for authentication patterns that rotate through accounts with timing delays designed to evade lockout. Track Windows Event ID 4625 aggregated by source IP with distinct target account counts. Use UEBA to detect deviations in authentication geography and timing.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Microsoft reported that Midnight Blizzard (Nobelium) used password spraying via residential proxies to compromise a legacy test tenant in January 2024. APT33 (Elfin) used password spraying against US and Saudi Arabian organizations. Citrix breach in 2019 began with password spraying.

attack_patterns.name: Brute Force: Credential Stuffing
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may use credentials obtained from breach dumps to attempt authentication against various services, exploiting password reuse across platforms. Credential stuffing differs from password spraying in that it uses known valid username-password pairs from previous breaches rather than guessing passwords. Automated tools like Sentry MBA, OpenBullet, and custom scripts are used to test millions of credential pairs against target services. This technique exploits the widespread practice of password reuse, with studies showing 60-65% of users reuse passwords across multiple services.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for high-volume authentication attempts from automated sources using bot detection (CAPTCHA, device fingerprinting, behavioral analysis). Alert on authentication attempts using credentials known to be in breach databases (integrate with Have I Been Pwned API or similar). Correlate successful authentications from new devices or locations following failed attempt patterns. Monitor for HTTP request patterns characteristic of credential stuffing tools (uniform User-Agent, rapid sequential requests, lack of JavaScript execution). Track abnormal login success rates compared to baseline.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The 2020 Nintendo breach affected 300,000 accounts via credential stuffing. Dunkin' Donuts was attacked twice (2015, 2018) using credential stuffing. Spotify experienced credential stuffing in 2020 affecting 350,000 accounts.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts to gain initial access, persistence, privilege escalation, or defense evasion. Compromised credentials may be used to bypass access controls and may even be used to gain persistent access to remote systems and externally available services. Valid accounts provide adversaries the ability to blend in with normal user activity, making detection significantly more difficult. This parent technique encompasses the use of default accounts, domain accounts, local accounts, and cloud accounts across all stages of an attack lifecycle.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Configure SIEM to baseline normal user behavior and alert on anomalies: unusual logon times, geographic impossibilities, new device registrations, and access to previously unused resources. Correlate Windows Event IDs 4624/4625 (logon success/failure) with 4648 (explicit credential logon) and 4672 (special privileges assigned). Monitor Azure AD for risky sign-ins using Identity Protection risk scores. Implement impossible travel detection using geo-IP correlation. Track service account usage for interactive logons which should not occur.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The SolarWinds Orion compromise (2020) used valid credentials obtained through supply chain compromise. The Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack (2021) began with a compromised VPN credential. The Uber breach (2022) started with purchased credentials from a contractor.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Default Accounts
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of default accounts to gain initial access or elevated privileges. Default accounts are built-in accounts that are pre-configured on systems, applications, network devices, and databases with well-known or documented usernames and passwords. Many organizations fail to change or disable these default credentials after deployment, leaving systems vulnerable to trivial compromise. Common examples include admin/admin, root/root, sa/sa (SQL Server), and default SNMP community strings.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Inventory all default accounts across the environment and monitor for their use. Alert on any authentication attempt using known default account names (admin, administrator, root, sa, guest, default). Monitor Windows Event ID 4624 for logons using built-in accounts (SID S-1-5-21-*-500, S-1-5-21-*-501). Scan network device configurations for default credentials using automated tools. Monitor for first-time logons from accounts that have been dormant since system deployment.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Mirai botnet (2016) propagated by using a list of 62 default username/password pairs for IoT devices. Multiple healthcare breaches have resulted from default credentials on medical devices.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of domain accounts to gain access to network resources. Domain accounts are managed by Active Directory Domain Services and provide access to resources across the Windows domain environment. Compromising domain accounts, especially those with administrative privileges, can provide broad access to the enterprise network. Adversaries may obtain domain credentials through credential dumping, keylogging, social engineering, or purchasing them on dark web marketplaces.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Active Directory authentication logs for anomalous logon patterns. Correlate Windows Event IDs 4768 (TGT request), 4769 (service ticket request), and 4624 (logon) for unusual access patterns. Implement Azure AD Identity Protection for hybrid environments. Alert on domain admin account logons to non-domain-controller systems. Track LDAP bind operations for credential harvesting patterns. Monitor for account logons from unusual source networks or during non-business hours using UEBA.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The NotPetya attack (2017) spread using compromised domain credentials via PsExec and WMI. APT29 (Cozy Bear) routinely compromises domain admin accounts for lateral movement.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Local Accounts
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of local accounts to gain initial access or move laterally within a network. Local accounts are configured on individual systems rather than managed by a central directory service. These accounts often share passwords across multiple systems in environments that lack centralized credential management, making compromise of one system potentially compromising many. Local administrator accounts are particularly valuable targets as they provide full control over the compromised host.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for local account authentication events particularly from network-based logons (Windows Event ID 4624 Logon Type 3, 10). Alert on local administrator account logons from unexpected source IPs. Track usage of the built-in local administrator account (RID 500). Monitor for pass-the-hash indicators: NTLM authentication (Event ID 4624 with LogonProcess NtLmSsp) from non-standard workstations. Deploy Microsoft LAPS and monitor for LAPS password retrieval events.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: WannaCry (2017) and Ryuk ransomware campaigns leveraged local accounts for lateral movement. Many ransomware operators use local admin accounts with shared passwords across systems.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of cloud accounts to gain initial access to cloud environments. Cloud accounts include Azure AD/Entra ID accounts, AWS IAM users, GCP service accounts, and SaaS application accounts. With the migration to cloud services, these accounts often provide access to critical business data and infrastructure. Cloud account compromise is particularly dangerous because it may bypass traditional network perimeter controls and provide access to data and services regardless of the attacker's physical location.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor cloud identity provider logs for anomalous sign-in activity. In Azure AD, enable Identity Protection and monitor for risky sign-ins (unfamiliar location, anonymous IP, leaked credentials, impossible travel). In AWS, monitor CloudTrail for ConsoleLogin events from unusual IPs and programmatic access (GetSessionToken, AssumeRole) patterns. Monitor for MFA bypass indicators and conditional access policy failures. Alert on new OAuth app consents and API permission grants. Track cloud account creation and role assignment changes.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Capital One breach (2019) exploited misconfigured IAM roles in AWS. Microsoft disclosed Midnight Blizzard (2024) accessed corporate email via compromised cloud test tenant. Uber breach (2022) began with compromised cloud contractor credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may steal web application session cookies to authenticate to web services without needing credentials. Session cookies are tokens that web applications use to maintain authenticated sessions between requests. By stealing these cookies through browser exploitation, malware, network interception, or XSS attacks, adversaries can impersonate legitimate users without knowing their passwords. Modern attacks focus on stealing session tokens from browser cookie stores, memory, or through adversary-in-the-middle proxies that intercept cookies during authentication.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for cookie theft indicators: unusual browser process behavior, access to browser cookie databases (Chrome: Cookies SQLite DB, Firefox: cookies.sqlite), and process injection into browser processes. Alert on session usage from IP addresses different from session establishment IP. Implement session binding to client fingerprints (IP, User-Agent, TLS fingerprint) and alert on mismatches. Monitor for AiTM proxy indicators in authentication flows (certificate mismatches, unusual redirect patterns). Track Azure AD for token replay detection events.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Evilginx2 and Modlishka are widely used AiTM proxy tools that steal session cookies. The 2022 Uber breach involved session cookie theft after social engineering. DEV-1101 phishing-as-a-service platform offers built-in cookie theft.

attack_patterns.name: Steal Application Access Token
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may steal application access tokens as a means of acquiring credentials to access remote systems and resources. Application access tokens, such as OAuth tokens, API keys, and bearer tokens, are used to authenticate and authorize requests to APIs and services. These tokens may be stored in configuration files, environment variables, browser local storage, or application databases. Token theft provides adversaries with the same level of access as the legitimate application or user, often bypassing multi-factor authentication since the token represents a completed authentication session.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for unauthorized access to token storage locations: environment variables, configuration files, browser localStorage/sessionStorage, and cloud metadata services. Alert on OAuth token usage from IP addresses different from the original token issuance. Track Azure AD for unusual application consent grants and token acquisition patterns. Monitor for mass token enumeration via cloud APIs (e.g., AWS STS GetCallerIdentity from unusual sources). Implement token binding and detect unbound token usage. Alert on tokens used after expected expiration through clock skew abuse.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: GitHub token exposure in public repositories has led to numerous supply chain compromises. The Codecov breach (2021) involved stealing CI/CD environment tokens. Slack tokens stolen from Rockstar Games (2022) led to GTA 6 leak.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may search compromised systems to find and obtain insecurely stored credentials. These credentials can be stored in many locations on a system including configuration files, scripts, source code, environment variables, and credential management utilities. Unsecured credentials are a pervasive problem across enterprise environments, particularly in DevOps pipelines where credentials are often embedded in code, CI/CD configurations, and infrastructure-as-code templates. This technique encompasses the discovery and collection of credentials from multiple storage locations.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Deploy file integrity monitoring on known credential storage locations (/etc/shadow, SAM hive, credential files). Monitor for suspicious file access patterns: processes reading multiple credential files in sequence. Use Gitleaks or truffleHog in CI/CD pipelines to detect credential commits. Alert on access to cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254) from unexpected processes. Monitor PowerShell and command-line for credential harvesting commands (findstr /si password, grep -r password). Track access to Windows Credential Manager via VaultCmd.exe or vaultcli.dll.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Uber breach (2016) involved AWS credentials stored in a private GitHub repository. Thousands of AWS keys are exposed daily on GitHub. The CircleCI breach (2023) exposed customer environment variables containing secrets.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may search local file systems and remote file shares for files containing insecurely stored credentials. Many applications and services store credentials in plaintext configuration files, scripts, or documentation. Common locations include web server configuration files (web.config, .env), application configuration (appsettings.json, database.yml), script files containing embedded passwords, and user home directories (.bashrc, .profile). Adversaries often use automated tools to scan for files matching patterns associated with credential storage.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor file access to known credential storage locations using file integrity monitoring (OSSEC, Wazuh, Sysmon Event ID 11). Alert on processes reading multiple configuration files containing sensitive keywords (password, secret, key, token). Track PowerShell commands like Get-Content, Select-String with credential-related search terms. Monitor for recursive file searches (dir /s, find, grep -r) targeting credential patterns. Deploy canary credentials in configuration files to detect access and usage.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Equifax breach (2017) involved credentials stored in a plaintext configuration file. AWS keys stored in .env files have been a recurring source of cloud account compromise.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Credentials in Registry
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may search the Windows Registry for credentials and passwords stored for other programs or services. The Windows Registry can contain credentials stored by applications, autologon configurations, VPN clients, and other software. Registry keys such as HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon (autologon), HKCU\Software\SimonTatham\PuTTY\Sessions (PuTTY saved sessions), and various VPN client keys store credentials that can be extracted by adversaries with appropriate access levels.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Windows Registry access to known credential storage keys using Sysmon Event ID 13 (Registry value set) and Event ID 12 (Registry key/value create/delete). Alert on reg.exe queries targeting credential-related keys (Winlogon, PuTTY, VPN clients). Track PowerShell Get-ItemProperty calls to sensitive registry paths. Monitor for automated registry enumeration tools like LaZagne or SharpDump accessing multiple credential registry locations in sequence.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT groups commonly harvest autologon credentials from Winlogon registry keys. PuTTY stored session credentials in the registry have been targeted in multiple intrusions.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Bash History
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may search bash command history files for insecurely typed credentials. Users sometimes pass credentials as command-line arguments when using tools like curl, mysql, ssh, or configuration scripts. These commands and their arguments are recorded in shell history files such as .bash_history, .zsh_history, and .history. Adversaries with access to a compromised system can read these history files to discover credentials, API keys, database passwords, and connection strings that were used in previous commands.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for access to shell history files (.bash_history, .zsh_history, .history) by processes other than the shell itself. Alert on cat, head, tail, or grep operations targeting history files. Implement file integrity monitoring on history files in user home directories. Configure HISTCONTROL=ignorespace to prevent recording commands prefixed with a space. Monitor for mass history file collection across multiple user accounts.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Numerous cloud security incidents have originated from AWS access keys typed in command-line arguments and recoverable from bash history. Linux server compromises frequently involve harvesting credentials from shell history.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Private Keys
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may search for private key certificate files on compromised systems to enable authentication, decryption, or digital signing. Private keys are used for SSH authentication, TLS/SSL certificates, code signing, encryption, and other cryptographic operations. Common file locations include ~/.ssh/id_rsa, /etc/ssl/private/, and Windows certificate stores. Unprotected private keys provide adversaries with persistent, password-less authentication to systems and services, and may enable impersonation of legitimate services through certificate abuse.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor file access to common private key locations (~/.ssh/, /etc/ssl/private/, certificate stores). Alert on processes reading multiple key files or copying key files to unusual locations. Track SSH key usage from unexpected source IPs by correlating authorized_keys modifications with SSH authentication logs. Monitor for certificate export operations using certutil.exe or Export-PfxCertificate. Implement HSM-based key storage and alert on software key access where HSM is expected.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Codecov supply chain attack (2021) exfiltrated SSH keys and credentials from CI/CD environments. SSH key compromise has been a common vector in cloud infrastructure breaches.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may attempt to access the cloud instance metadata API to collect credentials and other sensitive data. Cloud providers expose metadata services (AWS: 169.254.169.254, Azure: 169.254.169.254, GCP: metadata.google.internal) that provide temporary credentials for assigned IAM roles/managed identities. SSRF vulnerabilities in applications running on cloud instances can be exploited to access these metadata endpoints and steal temporary credentials. These stolen credentials provide the same permissions as the instance's IAM role.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for HTTP requests to cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254, metadata.google.internal) from application processes that should not access metadata. Configure AWS IMDSv2 enforcement and alert on IMDSv1 usage. Monitor for credential usage from IP addresses outside the expected cloud VPC. Alert on unusual STS:AssumeRole or STS:GetCallerIdentity calls following metadata access. Track Azure Managed Identity token acquisition from non-standard processes.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Capital One breach (2019) exploited SSRF to access AWS metadata API and steal IAM role credentials, affecting 106 million customers. This attack vector has been widely replicated across cloud environments.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Group Policy Preferences
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may attempt to find unsecured credentials in Group Policy Preferences (GPP). Prior to MS14-025, Group Policy Preferences allowed administrators to embed credentials in various policy settings, including local account password changes, mapped drive configurations, and scheduled task definitions. These credentials were encrypted with a publicly known AES key published by Microsoft, making them trivially decryptable. Although the vulnerability was patched in 2014, legacy GPP files containing credentials may persist in SYSVOL shares.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Scan SYSVOL shares for GPP XML files (Groups.xml, Services.xml, Scheduledtasks.xml, DataSources.xml, Printers.xml, Drives.xml) containing cpassword attributes. Monitor for access to \\domain\SYSVOL and \\domain\NETLOGON shares, particularly targeting GPP XML files. Alert on execution of tools like Get-GPPPassword (PowerSploit), gpp-decrypt, or gpprefdecrypt. Track Windows Event ID 5140/5145 for share access to SYSVOL targeting .xml files.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Despite MS14-025 patching in 2014, GPP credential harvesting remains common in penetration testing engagements. Many Active Directory environments still contain legacy GPP files with embedded credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Container API
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may gather credentials via APIs within a containers environment. Container orchestration platforms like Kubernetes expose APIs that may contain secrets, service account tokens, and other credentials. Kubernetes Secrets, while base64 encoded, are not encrypted by default at rest and can be accessed by any pod with the appropriate RBAC permissions. Docker daemon APIs, if exposed, can provide access to environment variables containing credentials. Improperly configured container environments frequently expose credentials through metadata, environment variables, and mounted secrets.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Kubernetes audit logs for Secret read operations (GET /api/v1/secrets, GET /api/v1/namespaces/*/secrets). Alert on service accounts accessing secrets outside their normal scope. Track Docker API access for container inspection operations that reveal environment variables. Monitor for kubectl commands listing or describing secrets. Alert on pods mounting all secrets or using broad RBAC permissions. Monitor for etcd direct access which would bypass Kubernetes RBAC.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Tesla's Kubernetes dashboard was exposed without authentication (2018), allowing attackers to access credentials stored in pods. Numerous container security incidents involve exposed Docker daemon APIs.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Chat Messages
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may directly collect unsecured credentials stored or passed through user communication services. Messaging platforms such as Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, and email are commonly used by employees to share credentials, API keys, connection strings, and other sensitive information. Once an adversary gains access to these platforms, they can search message history for credential-related keywords to discover shared secrets. This is particularly prevalent in DevOps and engineering teams that frequently share credentials through informal channels.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Implement DLP scanning on messaging platforms to detect credential patterns (API keys, connection strings, passwords). Monitor for bulk message history export or search operations across messaging platforms. Alert on Slack API calls for conversations.history or search.messages with credential-related queries. Track Microsoft Teams Graph API calls for message search operations. Monitor for unusual OAuth app permissions granted to messaging platforms.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The EA Games breach (2021) began with attackers purchasing stolen Slack cookies and finding credentials shared in Slack channels. The Rockstar Games breach (2022) similarly exploited Slack for credential discovery.

attack_patterns.name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may attempt to subvert Kerberos authentication by stealing or forging Kerberos tickets to enable lateral movement and persistence. Kerberos is the default authentication protocol in Active Directory environments and relies on ticket-granting tickets (TGTs) and service tickets (TGSs) for authentication. By obtaining or forging these tickets, adversaries can authenticate as any user or service without knowing their password. This technique family includes Golden Ticket, Silver Ticket, Kerberoasting, and AS-REP Roasting attacks, each targeting different aspects of the Kerberos protocol.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for anomalous Kerberos authentication activity. Alert on Windows Event IDs 4768 (TGT requests), 4769 (service ticket requests), and 4770 (ticket renewals) with unusual properties. Detect Golden Tickets by monitoring for TGTs with abnormally long lifetimes or issued outside normal KDC operations. Track Kerberoasting via Event ID 4769 with encryption type 0x17 (RC4-HMAC) for service accounts. Monitor for AS-REP Roasting via Event ID 4768 with result code 0x0 for accounts with pre-authentication disabled. Use Advanced Threat Analytics or Microsoft Defender for Identity.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 used Golden Tickets for persistent access during the SolarWinds campaign. Operation Wocao used Kerberoasting and Silver Tickets for lateral movement. Multiple ransomware operators use Kerberoasting as a standard lateral movement technique.

attack_patterns.name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Golden Ticket
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may forge Kerberos ticket-granting tickets (TGTs) using the Key Distribution Center (KDC) service account (KRBTGT) hash to authenticate as any user with any privileges in the Active Directory domain. A Golden Ticket provides complete and persistent domain access because the forged TGT is indistinguishable from a legitimate one. The KRBTGT hash changes only when explicitly rotated, meaning a Golden Ticket can persist through password resets of individual accounts. This represents the highest level of Active Directory compromise, providing unlimited lateral movement and persistence.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for TGTs with abnormally long lifetimes (default maximum is 10 hours; Golden Tickets often have 10-year lifetimes). Alert on Windows Event ID 4769 for service ticket requests where the TGT has anomalous properties. Detect forged TGTs by enabling Kerberos PAC validation and monitoring for PAC-less tickets. Monitor for KRBTGT account password hash extraction via DCSync (Event ID 4662 with objectType for domain replication). Implement Microsoft Defender for Identity which detects Golden Ticket usage. Rotate KRBTGT password twice in rapid succession to invalidate all existing tickets.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 (Cozy Bear) used Golden Tickets during the SolarWinds breach for persistent domain access. The technique was first demonstrated publicly by Benjamin Delpy in the Mimikatz toolkit.

attack_patterns.name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Silver Ticket
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may forge Kerberos service tickets (TGSs) using the service account's NTLM hash to access specific services without contacting the Key Distribution Center (KDC). Unlike Golden Tickets, Silver Tickets are scoped to specific services (e.g., CIFS, HTTP, MSSQL) and do not require domain controller interaction, making them harder to detect through centralized logging. The forged TGS is presented directly to the target service, which validates it using the service account's key. Silver Tickets are particularly dangerous because they bypass many traditional Kerberos monitoring controls.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Silver Ticket detection is challenging because the forged ticket is used directly with the target service, not the KDC. Monitor for service access events (Windows Event ID 4624 Logon Type 3) that lack corresponding TGS request events (Event ID 4769) on domain controllers. Enable Kerberos service ticket validation using PAC validation and alert on tickets without valid PAC signatures. Monitor for AES vs RC4 encryption downgrade in service tickets. Use Microsoft Defender for Identity to detect anomalous service ticket presentations.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Operation Wocao used Silver Tickets for stealthy lateral movement. Multiple APT groups have used Silver Tickets to access specific services while evading Golden Ticket detection.

attack_patterns.name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may abuse Kerberos service ticket requests to obtain service account password hashes for offline cracking. Any authenticated domain user can request a Kerberos service ticket (TGS) for any service principal name (SPN) registered in Active Directory. The returned ticket is encrypted with the service account's NTLM hash, which can then be extracted and subjected to offline brute force or dictionary attacks. This is particularly effective against service accounts with weak passwords and when SPN is configured on user accounts rather than machine accounts, as machine account passwords are randomly generated and 120+ characters.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Windows Event ID 4769 (TGS request) for requests with RC4 encryption type (0x17) targeting service accounts, especially when a single user requests tickets for multiple SPNs in a short time. Alert when a single account requests TGS tickets for more than 3 distinct service accounts within 10 minutes. Track PowerShell execution of known Kerberoasting tools (Invoke-Kerberoast, Rubeus kerberoast). Monitor for unusual LDAP queries enumerating SPNs (servicePrincipalName attribute). Use Microsoft Defender for Identity Kerberoasting detection.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Kerberoasting is one of the most commonly used techniques by ransomware operators including REvil, Conti, and LockBit. FIN7 has used Kerberoasting extensively in financial sector intrusions.

attack_patterns.name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: AS-REP Roasting
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may reveal credentials of accounts that have disabled Kerberos pre-authentication. When Kerberos pre-authentication is disabled for an account (DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH flag), any user can send an AS-REQ request for that account and receive an AS-REP response encrypted with the account's password hash, which can then be cracked offline. This technique requires less privilege than Kerberoasting since it does not require domain authentication to exploit externally. Accounts with pre-authentication disabled are relatively rare but represent a significant security risk when present.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Windows Event ID 4768 (TGT request) for accounts with pre-authentication disabled (result code 0x0 without pre-auth data). Alert on LDAP queries filtering for userAccountControl attribute with DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH flag (0x400000). Track PowerShell execution of AS-REP Roasting tools (Get-ASREPHash, Rubeus asreproast). Monitor for bulk AS-REQ requests without pre-authentication data. Regularly audit Active Directory for accounts with pre-authentication disabled using Get-ADUser -Filter {DoesNotRequirePreAuth -eq $True}.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: AS-REP Roasting has been used by APT groups including APT40 and various ransomware operators as a low-noise credential harvesting technique.

attack_patterns.name: Forge Web Credentials
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may forge credential materials that can be used to gain access to web applications or Internet services. Web authentication technologies such as cookies, SAML assertions, OAuth tokens, and JWT claims rely on cryptographic signing or encryption to ensure integrity. If adversaries obtain the signing keys or secrets used to generate these credentials, they can forge arbitrary credential material to impersonate any user. This technique differs from credential theft in that the adversary creates new credential material rather than stealing existing tokens. The impact is often more severe as forged credentials may grant access beyond what any single stolen credential would provide.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for web credential usage patterns that deviate from normal: sessions created without corresponding authentication events, tokens with unusual claims or lifetimes, and SAML assertions from unexpected issuers. Alert on authentication events that bypass the normal login flow. Monitor for access to signing key material (certificates, HMAC secrets) used for token generation. Track changes to federation trust configurations and SAML IdP metadata. Implement token introspection and validation logging.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The SolarWinds attack (2020) used forged SAML tokens (Golden SAML) to access cloud resources. The Microsoft Exchange Hailfstorm attack involved forged authentication tokens.

attack_patterns.name: Forge Web Credentials: Web Cookies
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may forge web cookies to gain access to web applications without valid credentials. Web applications use cookies to maintain session state and store authentication information. If an adversary can obtain or derive the secret key used to generate session cookies (such as the ASP.NET machineKey, Django SECRET_KEY, or Flask secret_key), they can forge cookies that grant authenticated access as any user. This differs from cookie theft (T1539) in that the adversary creates new cookies rather than stealing existing ones, potentially providing access to any account including administrative accounts.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for session cookies that appear without corresponding authentication events in application logs. Alert on sessions associated with user accounts that have not recently logged in. Implement session logging that records cookie creation and correlate with authentication events. Monitor for access to application secret key configuration files. Track cookie usage patterns that deviate from user behavioral baselines (access time, IP, resources accessed).
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Multiple web application frameworks have been targeted for cookie forging. ASP.NET machineKey exposure has led to cookie forging attacks. The Outlook Web Application (OWA) has been targeted with forged authentication cookies.

attack_patterns.name: Forge Web Credentials: SAML Tokens
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may forge SAML tokens with any claims and lifetimes if they possess the signing certificate, enabling persistent access to all services that trust the SAML identity provider. Known as a 'Golden SAML' attack, this technique allows the adversary to impersonate any user for any SAML-federated application without needing the user's credentials or triggering normal authentication logging. The attack requires the SAML token signing certificate private key, which is typically stored on the AD FS server or in the identity provider configuration. This provides the adversary with persistent, stealthy access that survives password resets and MFA changes.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for SAML token usage without corresponding authentication events at the identity provider. Alert on SAML assertions with unusual lifetimes, claims, or issuer information. Track access to AD FS signing certificates and configuration databases. Monitor for SAML token replays (same assertion used multiple times). Implement certificate rotation for SAML signing keys and alert on signing key access. In Azure AD, monitor for changes to federation settings and trusted domains. Use Microsoft Defender for Identity for Golden SAML detection.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The SolarWinds/NOBELIUM attack (2020) is the most prominent example of Golden SAML, where attackers stole the AD FS signing certificate to forge SAML tokens accessing Microsoft 365 and Azure resources. CyberArk first described the Golden SAML technique in 2017.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify authentication mechanisms and processes to access user credentials or enable otherwise unwarranted access to accounts. By modifying the authentication process, adversaries can reveal credentials of accounts, create backdoor access, or bypass authentication entirely. This includes modifying domain controllers, pluggable authentication modules, password filters, network device authentication, and multi-factor authentication implementations. These modifications can provide persistent, stealthy credential access that survives normal security remediation steps.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for unauthorized changes to authentication-related system components: DLL modifications on domain controllers, PAM configuration changes, password filter DLL registrations, and network device firmware modifications. Alert on Windows Event ID 4657 (registry value modification) for authentication-related registry keys. Monitor file integrity of authentication libraries (/lib/security/, lsass.exe dependencies). Track changes to AD FS configurations, MFA provider settings, and federation trust relationships. Implement code signing validation for authentication components.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 modified AD FS authentication during the SolarWinds compromise (FoggyWeb and MagicWeb malware). The FIN11 group modified Pulse Secure VPN authentication modules. Operation Cloud Hopper modified authentication processes on managed service provider systems.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Domain Controller Authentication
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may patch the authentication process on a domain controller to bypass the typical authentication mechanisms and enable access to accounts. Malicious modifications to the domain controller authentication process can include patching the LSASS process in memory to accept any password, installing a skeleton key that allows authentication with a universal password while preserving normal user access, or modifying the NTDS.dit to add backdoor credentials. The Skeleton Key malware is a well-known example that patches LSASS to accept a master password for any domain account.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor domain controller LSASS process for memory modifications using Sysmon Event ID 10 (ProcessAccess with GrantedAccess to lsass.exe). Alert on unusual DLLs loaded by lsass.exe (Sysmon Event ID 7). Monitor for Skeleton Key indicators: Event ID 7045 (new service installed) and Event ID 4673 (privileged service called). Track ntdsutil.exe and ntds.dit file access. Implement credential guard on domain controllers. Monitor for authentication anomalies where the same password hash authenticates multiple accounts.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Skeleton Key malware discovered in 2015 patched domain controller LSASS to accept a master password. APT groups have used DC patching for persistent access in government and defense sector intrusions.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Password Filter DLL
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may register malicious password filter DLLs in the authentication process to capture plaintext credentials. Windows allows administrators to register custom password filter DLLs that are called whenever a password change occurs, receiving the new password in plaintext for validation. By registering a malicious DLL, adversaries can capture every password change in the domain in cleartext. The DLL is registered via the HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Notification Packages registry value and is loaded by lsass.exe on the domain controller.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor the registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Notification Packages for new entries using Sysmon Event ID 13. Alert on any new DLLs loaded by lsass.exe on domain controllers (Sysmon Event ID 7). Verify the code signing and integrity of all registered password filter DLLs. Monitor for new DLL files placed in the System32 directory on domain controllers. Track registry modifications to LSA configuration using Windows Event ID 4657.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Multiple APT groups have used password filter DLLs to harvest credentials from domain controllers. The technique has been documented in nation-state operations targeting government networks.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Pluggable Authentication Modules
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify pluggable authentication modules (PAM) to access user credentials or enable otherwise unwarranted access to accounts. PAM is a modular authentication system used in Linux and Unix systems that allows system administrators to configure authentication policies. By modifying PAM modules or their configuration files in /etc/pam.d/, adversaries can capture plaintext credentials during authentication, create backdoor accounts that bypass normal authentication, or weaken authentication requirements. Modified PAM modules persist across reboots and are difficult to detect without file integrity monitoring.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Implement file integrity monitoring on PAM module files (/lib/security/, /lib64/security/) and configuration files (/etc/pam.d/). Alert on any modification to PAM shared objects or configuration files. Monitor for new or modified files in PAM directories. Track process execution of compilers (gcc) on systems where PAM modifications are detected. Compare PAM module checksums against known-good baselines from package managers. Monitor syslog for PAM authentication events with unusual patterns.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Linux PAM backdoors have been used by multiple threat actors including Ebury and Windigo operations. APT groups targeting government Linux infrastructure have modified PAM for persistent credential harvesting.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Network Device Authentication
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may use patch system image to hardcode a password in the operating system of network devices, creating a backdoor with root-level access to the device. Network devices such as routers, switches, and firewalls run embedded operating systems that handle authentication locally or through RADIUS/TACACS+. By modifying the device firmware or operating system image, adversaries can install persistent authentication backdoors that survive configuration resets. These modifications can also disable authentication logging, making the backdoor invisible to monitoring systems.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Implement firmware integrity verification using Cisco IOS Secure Boot or equivalent features. Compare running firmware hashes against known-good images from the vendor. Monitor for unexpected firmware uploads or reloads. Alert on TFTP/SCP transfers of IOS images to network devices. Track SNMP and CLI configuration changes to authentication settings. Monitor TACACS+/RADIUS logs for authentication events from unexpected device IPs. Implement network device configuration management and drift detection.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The SYNful Knock attack discovered by Mandiant in 2015 modified Cisco IOS firmware to create persistent backdoors. Nation-state actors have modified network device firmware in multiple campaigns targeting ISPs and government networks.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Reversible Encryption
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may abuse the AllowReversiblePasswordEncryption Active Directory attribute to obtain cleartext passwords. When this attribute is enabled for a user account, their password is stored using reversible encryption in the NTDS.dit database, effectively storing it in a recoverable format. Adversaries with sufficient privileges can enable this attribute on target accounts and then recover the plaintext password after the user's next password change. This technique is particularly insidious because it appears as a legitimate configuration change.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for changes to the AllowReversiblePasswordEncryption attribute on user accounts via Windows Event ID 5136 (directory service object modified) and Event ID 4738 (user account changed). Alert on any account where this attribute is enabled, as it is almost never legitimately required. Regularly audit AD for accounts with this attribute set using Get-ADUser -Filter {AllowReversiblePasswordEncryption -eq $True}. Monitor for DCSync operations following attribute changes.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: This technique is commonly discovered during Active Directory security assessments. While less common in real-world attacks compared to other credential access methods, it represents a persistent configuration-based backdoor.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Multi-Factor Authentication Interception
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may target multi-factor authentication (MFA) mechanisms to gain access to credentials that can be used to access systems, services, and network resources. By modifying the MFA process at the identity provider level, adversaries can bypass or intercept MFA challenges. This includes modifying AD FS configurations to skip MFA for specific conditions, manipulating conditional access policies, or installing rogue MFA providers that capture one-time codes. Unlike MFA fatigue (T1621), this technique involves modifying the authentication infrastructure itself rather than exhausting the user.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for changes to MFA provider configurations and conditional access policies. Alert on modifications to AD FS claim rules, Azure AD Conditional Access policies, and MFA provider settings. Track registration of new MFA methods for user accounts, especially for privileged accounts. Monitor for changes to trusted IP ranges or named locations that could weaken MFA requirements. Alert on bulk MFA method modifications across multiple accounts. Track Azure AD audit logs for MFA bypass events.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 modified AD FS to bypass MFA during the SolarWinds compromise using FoggyWeb and MagicWeb malware. Storm-0558 bypassed Microsoft MFA in 2023 using forged authentication tokens.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Hybrid Identity
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify hybrid identity environments to gain persistent access by manipulating the synchronization between on-premises and cloud identity systems. Hybrid identity solutions like Azure AD Connect synchronize user accounts, passwords, and group memberships between on-premises Active Directory and cloud directories. By compromising the synchronization service, adversaries can modify synchronized credentials, inject backdoor accounts into cloud environments, or intercept password hashes during synchronization. The Azure AD Connect service account has significant privileges in both environments, making it a high-value target.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Azure AD Connect server for unauthorized access and configuration changes. Alert on modifications to sync rules, connector configurations, and the Azure AD Connect database (ADSync). Track Azure AD Connect service account activity for anomalous operations. Monitor for changes to password hash synchronization (PHS) or pass-through authentication (PTA) configurations. Alert on new agent registrations for PTA or cloud provisioning. Track Azure AD audit logs for synchronized account modifications that don't match expected sync cycles.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Multiple APT groups have targeted Azure AD Connect servers for hybrid identity compromise. MERCURY (MuddyWater) has targeted Azure AD Connect in campaigns against Middle Eastern organizations. The AADInternals toolkit demonstrates various hybrid identity attack techniques.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Network Provider DLL
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may register a rogue network provider DLL to capture cleartext user credentials during the Windows logon process. Network provider DLLs are called during Windows authentication to handle network logon operations. By registering a malicious DLL as a network provider through the registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\NetworkProvider\Order, adversaries can intercept credentials in cleartext as they are passed through the authentication chain. This technique is stealthier than credential dumping tools because it captures credentials passively during normal authentication rather than requiring active memory access.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor the registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\NetworkProvider\Order for new or modified entries using Sysmon Event ID 13. Alert on new DLL files registered as network providers. Verify all registered network provider DLLs against known-good baselines and validate their digital signatures. Monitor for DLL loading events (Sysmon Event ID 7) in the mpnotify.exe process. Track file creation events in System32 directory for new DLLs. Regularly audit registered network providers using PowerShell.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The NPPSpy tool demonstrates this technique for credential harvesting. Several APT groups have used network provider DLLs for passive credential collection on compromised workstations and servers.

attack_patterns.name: Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may attempt to bypass multi-factor authentication (MFA) mechanisms by generating repeated push notifications or phone calls to the target user, a technique known as MFA fatigue or MFA bombing. After obtaining valid credentials through other means (phishing, credential stuffing, or purchase), the adversary repeatedly triggers MFA prompts hoping the user will approve one either accidentally, out of frustration, or confusion. This technique exploits human factors rather than technical vulnerabilities in the MFA implementation. Organizations using simple push-based MFA without number matching or additional context are most vulnerable.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for high volumes of MFA push notifications sent to a single user in a short time period. Alert on 3+ MFA prompts within 10 minutes for the same user without a successful authentication. Correlate MFA prompt generation with failed authentication attempts from unusual IPs. Monitor Azure AD for riskEventType 'anomalousToken' and repeated MFA denial events. Track Microsoft Authenticator app usage for approval events that follow rapid denial sequences. Alert on successful MFA approval following a series of denials, especially outside business hours.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Uber breach (September 2022) was accomplished via MFA fatigue against an Uber contractor. Lapsus$ group extensively used MFA fatigue attacks against Microsoft, Okta, and other targets in 2022. Cisco was breached in May 2022 via MFA fatigue targeting an employee's personal Google account.

attack_patterns.name: Multi-Factor Authentication Interception
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may target multi-factor authentication mechanisms (smart cards, token generators, etc.) to gain access to credentials that can be used to access systems, services, and network resources. This technique involves intercepting or stealing physical or digital MFA tokens, including hardware tokens (RSA SecurID, YubiKey), software tokens (TOTP generators), SMS codes, and smart card credentials. Unlike MFA bypass or fatigue attacks, this technique directly targets the MFA credential itself through malware on the user's device, physical theft, SIM swapping, or real-time phishing proxies that capture OTP codes as the user enters them.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for SIM swap indicators: sudden loss of cellular service followed by authentication from new device. Alert on MFA method changes (new phone number, new authenticator app registration) for privileged accounts. Track real-time phishing proxy indicators: authentication sessions where MFA code is used from a different IP than where it was generated. Monitor for hardware token cloning indicators: same token serial used from geographically impossible locations. Alert on smart card authentication events from unexpected workstations.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The RSA SecurID breach (2011) compromised token seeds affecting major defense contractors. SIM swapping attacks have targeted cryptocurrency holders and high-profile individuals. Evilginx2 real-time phishing proxy intercepts MFA codes during authentication.

attack_patterns.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may attempt to position themselves between two or more networked devices to support credential access and data collection. By abusing features of common networking protocols that can determine the flow of network traffic (e.g., ARP, DNS, LLMNR, mDNS, NBNS, WPAD, DHCPv6), adversaries may force a device to communicate through an adversary-controlled system to collect authentication credentials. Adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) attacks in the IAM context are particularly dangerous because they can intercept complete authentication flows including MFA tokens, session cookies, and OAuth authorization codes.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor network traffic for ARP spoofing indicators (duplicate IP-MAC mappings, gratuitous ARP). Alert on LLMNR/NBNS/mDNS response traffic from non-DNS servers. Monitor for WPAD configuration changes and unauthorized proxy configurations. Detect AiTM phishing proxies by monitoring for certificate mismatches and unexpected redirect chains during authentication. Track DNS responses that differ from expected authoritative answers. Monitor for Responder and Inveigh tool signatures on the network.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: AiTM phishing campaigns using Evilginx2 have been observed targeting Microsoft 365 organizations to steal session cookies and bypass MFA. DEV-1101 (Storm-1101) operates a phishing-as-a-service platform with AiTM capabilities.

attack_patterns.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may spoof an authoritative source for name resolution to force communication through an adversary-controlled system, enabling credential interception. Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution (LLMNR) and NetBIOS Name Service (NBT-NS) are fallback name resolution protocols used in Windows environments when DNS resolution fails. By responding to these broadcast requests with poisoned responses, adversaries can redirect authentication attempts to their systems and capture NTLMv2 hashes or relay authentication to other services. This technique is extremely effective in internal networks that have not disabled these legacy protocols.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for LLMNR (UDP 5355) and NBT-NS (UDP 137) traffic on the network. Alert on LLMNR/NBT-NS responses from non-DNS servers. Detect tools like Responder and Inveigh by monitoring for characteristic response patterns. Monitor Windows Event ID 4624 for NTLM authentication (NtLmSsp) from unexpected source IPs. Track SMB relay indicators: authentication events where the source IP in the network packet differs from the workstation name in the authentication event. Implement SMB signing enforcement and monitor for unsigned SMB sessions.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning is one of the most commonly exploited techniques in internal penetration tests. The Responder tool is used extensively by both penetration testers and real-world attackers for credential harvesting in corporate networks.

attack_patterns.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: ARP Cache Poisoning
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may poison Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) caches to position themselves between the communication of two or more networked devices, enabling credential interception. ARP is used to resolve IP addresses to MAC addresses on local networks. By sending spoofed ARP replies, adversaries can associate their MAC address with the IP of a default gateway or another host, causing traffic intended for that IP to be sent to the adversary instead. This enables interception of authentication traffic, credential harvesting, and session hijacking on the local network segment.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for ARP cache poisoning indicators: duplicate IP-to-MAC mappings, frequent ARP table changes, and gratuitous ARP packets. Deploy Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on switches and alert on violations. Monitor for tools like arpspoof, ettercap, and Bettercap by their network signatures. Track ARP table changes on critical infrastructure devices. Implement DHCP snooping and monitor for unauthorized DHCP responses. Alert on MAC address changes for gateway IPs in ARP tables.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: ARP cache poisoning is a foundational technique for man-in-the-middle attacks on local networks. It has been used in targeted attacks against financial institutions and government networks to intercept authentication credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: DHCP Spoofing
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may redirect network traffic to adversary-controlled systems by spoofing Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) responses. By setting up a rogue DHCP server or exploiting race conditions with legitimate DHCP servers, adversaries can provide clients with malicious network configuration including rogue DNS servers, default gateways, and WPAD proxy settings. This enables interception of authentication traffic, DNS hijacking for credential harvesting, and man-in-the-middle attacks on authentication flows. DHCP spoofing can affect all new devices joining the network segment.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for rogue DHCP servers using DHCP snooping on network switches. Alert on DHCP responses from unauthorized MAC addresses or IPs. Track DHCP lease assignments for unexpected DNS server or gateway configurations. Monitor for rapid DHCP lease exhaustion that could indicate a starvation attack preceding spoofing. Alert on WPAD configuration changes delivered via DHCP options. Monitor DNS query patterns for resolution through unauthorized DNS servers.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: DHCP spoofing has been used in targeted attacks to redirect traffic through adversary-controlled infrastructure. Combined with DNS spoofing, it enables credential harvesting at scale on local networks.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials from Password Stores
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may search for common password storage locations to obtain user credentials. Passwords are stored in several places on a system depending on the operating system or application. Common password stores include web browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge), operating system credential managers (Windows Credential Manager, macOS Keychain, GNOME Keyring), password management applications (KeePass, LastPass, 1Password), and cloud-synced credential stores. Access to these stores can provide credentials for numerous accounts and services, enabling broad lateral movement and account takeover.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for access to credential store files and databases: Chrome Login Data SQLite database, Firefox logins.json, Windows Credential Manager (vaultcmd.exe, VaultSvc). Alert on processes reading browser credential databases other than the browser itself. Track access to KeePass database files (.kdbx). Monitor for credential dump tools (LaZagne, SharpChrome, HackBrowserData) execution. Alert on suspicious access to macOS Keychain via security command-line tool. Monitor Windows Event ID 5379 (credential manager read).
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The RedLine Stealer malware family is widely used to extract credentials from browser password stores. Raccoon Stealer and Vidar target browser credential databases. Multiple APT groups use credential store harvesting as a standard post-exploitation step.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials from Password Stores: Credentials from Web Browsers
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may acquire credentials from web browsers by reading browser-specific credential storage files. Web browsers commonly save user credentials (usernames and passwords) for websites to enable auto-fill functionality. These credentials are stored in browser-specific databases: Chrome uses the Login Data SQLite database encrypted with DPAPI on Windows and the Keychain on macOS, Firefox stores credentials in logins.json encrypted with the browser's master key. Tools like LaZagne, SharpChrome, and HackBrowserData can automate the extraction and decryption of these stored credentials.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for processes other than web browsers accessing browser credential storage files (Chrome: %LocalAppData%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Login Data, Firefox: %AppData%\Mozilla\Firefox\Profiles\*.default\logins.json). Alert on file read events for browser credential databases from non-browser processes using Sysmon Event ID 11. Track DPAPI CryptUnprotectData calls from unexpected processes (Event ID 4693). Monitor for known credential extraction tools by process name, hash, or command-line arguments.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: RedLine Stealer, Raccoon Stealer, and Vidar are widely distributed malware families that specifically target browser credential stores. The Lapsus$ group harvested browser credentials from compromised employee devices.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials from Password Stores: Password Managers
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may acquire credentials stored in third-party password managers such as KeePass, LastPass, 1Password, Bitwarden, and Dashlane. These applications store encrypted vaults containing credentials for many accounts and services. Adversaries may target the master password through keylogging, clipboard monitoring, or memory scraping, or they may directly attack the encrypted vault file. Compromising a password manager can provide access to hundreds of credentials simultaneously, making it a high-value target for adversaries seeking broad access.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for access to password manager vault files (.kdbx for KeePass, vault databases for other managers) by processes other than the password manager application. Alert on clipboard access patterns consistent with credential extraction. Track memory access to password manager processes using Sysmon Event ID 10. Monitor for KeePass plugin installation (potential backdoor) and configuration changes. Alert on password manager vault file copies to unusual locations or network shares.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The LastPass breach (2022) resulted in encrypted password vaults being stolen. A KeePass vulnerability (CVE-2023-32784) allowed master password extraction from process memory. The Lapsus$ group targeted password managers on compromised devices.

attack_patterns.name: Input Capture: Keylogging
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may log user keystrokes to intercept credentials as the user types them. Keylogging is one of the most fundamental credential harvesting techniques, capturing passwords, PINs, and other sensitive data entered via keyboard. Keyloggers can be implemented as kernel-mode drivers, user-mode API hooks (SetWindowsHookEx), hardware devices, or browser extensions. Modern keyloggers often include form-grabbing capabilities that capture web form data before it is encrypted for transmission, defeating TLS protection for credential capture.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for API calls associated with keylogging: SetWindowsHookEx with WH_KEYBOARD_LL hook type, GetAsyncKeyState in loops, and Raw Input API registration. Alert on Sysmon Event ID 1 (Process Create) for known keylogger binaries and suspicious programs with keyboard hook capabilities. Monitor for unusual DLL injections into user processes (Sysmon Event ID 8). Track new browser extensions with input monitoring permissions. Detect hardware keyloggers through USB device inventory monitoring and physical inspections.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: KeyBoy malware used by APT groups targeting government entities included keylogging functionality. Olympic Destroyer malware used keylogging for credential harvesting. The Agent Tesla RAT includes comprehensive keylogging capabilities.

attack_patterns.name: Forced Authentication
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may gather credential material by invoking or forcing a user to automatically provide authentication information through a mechanism in which they can intercept. Forced authentication techniques cause the victim's system to automatically send authentication credentials (typically NTLM hashes) to an adversary-controlled server. Common methods include placing UNC paths in documents, shortcut files (.lnk, .scf, .url), and web pages that trigger SMB authentication to remote servers. The captured NTLMv2 hashes can then be cracked offline or relayed to other services for unauthorized access.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for outbound SMB traffic (TCP 445) to external or unusual internal IP addresses. Alert on NTLM authentication events (Windows Event ID 4624 with NTLM authentication) to non-standard destinations. Monitor for creation of files containing UNC paths (.lnk, .url, .scf, .docx with external references). Track DNS queries for potential Responder/Inveigh servers. Monitor email attachments and shared documents for embedded UNC path references. Alert on outbound WebDAV traffic to untrusted destinations.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT28 (Fancy Bear) used .lnk files in phishing emails to force NTLM authentication for credential harvesting. Multiple threat actors use this technique in combination with Responder for internal network credential harvesting.

attack_patterns.name: Network Sniffing
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may sniff network traffic to capture credentials and other sensitive information passing over the network. Network sniffing is particularly effective for capturing credentials transmitted in cleartext or weakly protected protocols such as HTTP Basic Auth, FTP, Telnet, LDAP (without TLS), SMTP, POP3, and IMAP. Even in environments using encrypted protocols, misconfigurations such as SSL stripping, certificate validation bypasses, or legacy protocol support can expose credentials to network capture. Adversaries may use tools like Wireshark, tcpdump, or specialized credential sniffing tools.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for network interface promiscuous mode changes using host-based monitoring. Alert on processes using raw sockets or packet capture libraries (pcap). Detect network sniffing tools by process name (tcpdump, tshark, dumpcap) and library loading (npcap, libpcap). Monitor for promiscuous mode on non-monitoring network interfaces. Track Windows Event ID 4656 (handle requested to network adapter in raw mode). Monitor for packet capture file creation (.pcap, .pcapng) on non-analyst workstations.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT groups routinely deploy network sniffers after initial compromise to harvest credentials from internal network traffic. The DarkHotel APT used network sniffing to capture hotel Wi-Fi credentials.

attack_patterns.name: OS Credential Dumping
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password, from the operating system and software. Credentials can be obtained from OS caches, memory, or structures. OS credential dumping is one of the most impactful credential access techniques because it can reveal passwords and hashes for many accounts simultaneously, including service accounts and privileged accounts. Tools like Mimikatz, ProcDump, and comsvcs.dll are commonly used to extract credentials from LSASS memory, SAM database, NTDS.dit, and other credential stores.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for access to LSASS process memory (Sysmon Event ID 10 with TargetImage lsass.exe and GrantedAccess 0x1010 or 0x1410). Alert on suspicious processes accessing lsass.exe including unsigned binaries, PowerShell, rundll32, and comsvcs.dll. Monitor for SAM database access (reg save HKLM\SAM). Track ntdsutil.exe and ntds.dit file access on domain controllers. Monitor for procdump.exe and other dump tools targeting lsass. Implement Credential Guard and Protected Process Light for LSASS. Alert on volume shadow copy creation targeting system drives.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Mimikatz has been used by virtually every major threat actor since 2011. The NotPetya attack used credential dumping for lateral movement. APT29, APT28, FIN7, and most ransomware operators use OS credential dumping as a standard technique.

attack_patterns.name: OS Credential Dumping: DCSync
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may attempt to access credentials and other sensitive information by abusing a Windows Domain Controller's application programming interface (API) to simulate the replication process from a remote domain controller using a technique called DCSync. DCSync abuses the Directory Replication Service (DRS) Remote Protocol to request replication of credential data from a domain controller, effectively requesting password hashes for any account in the domain. This requires the attacker to have the Replicating Directory Changes and Replicating Directory Changes All privileges, typically held by Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and the domain controller computer accounts.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for directory replication service access requests from non-domain-controller sources. Alert on Windows Event ID 4662 with ObjectType matching domain replication GUIDs (1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2 and 1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2) from non-DC source addresses. Track Windows Event ID 4624 for logon events from accounts with replication privileges. Monitor for accounts being granted Replicating Directory Changes permissions (Event ID 5136). Use Microsoft Defender for Identity for DCSync detection.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: DCSync was used in the SolarWinds compromise to extract the KRBTGT hash for Golden Ticket creation. Most ransomware operators and APT groups use DCSync as part of Active Directory compromise. Mimikatz lsadump::dcsync is the most common implementation.
